Patch Set 9: Code-Review+1

the approach is still confusing me.. first of all, we can't detect an issue until LFSCK. then LFSCK itself can take a lot. then OST can be unavailable for the new files for long. and all this is to be able to generate unique IDs? we do have notion of sequence. it's easy and cheap to start to use a new sequence and make sure IDs are unique? and OFD itself can easily detect an issue if the precreation process meet an existing object (which is cheap as well). and then we don't need very special interactions between OFD and LFSCK.

Patch Set 9:

Alex: according to former suggestion, if the LFSCK found inconsistent LAST_ID, then it will make the OST to be read-only. That is current implementation. In theory, your way of using new sequence under such case can avoid the read-only; but from the implementation view, there are some detailed issues to be considered:

1) For most of cases, the system will not be in inconsistent status, but the OFD also needs to handle carefully to avoid misguide the LFSCK to regard the system to be inconsistent, such as update the LAST_ID before (pre-)create the OST-objects.

2) From the OFD view, it is not always easily to detect the inconsistency by itself. For example, if the LAST_ID file crashed, and the OFD in handling the initialisation LAST_ID sync with MDT, then the expected "orphan cleanup" may become "pre-create". On the other hand, according to our recovery/replay mechanism, we allow the write relay to re-create OST-object. So even if the OST-object exists, the OFD-create cannot simply say that the system in inconsistency status.

3) When the LFSCK found inconsistency, it does not know whether it is the unique crashed LAST_ID file or there may be other ones until if finished the whole device scanning. So even if the OFD uses new sequence under inconsistency status, but if the LFSCK is paused before completed and resumes after the OST restart, it is also cannot easily to know whether the new allocated sequence is still valid to be used or not.

There may be other corner cases to be considered also. So compared with current implementation, it seems not simplify the logic and save interactions between OFD and LFSCK too much.

Patch Set 9:

> For most of cases, the system will not be in inconsistent status, but the OFD also needs to handle carefully to avoid misguide the LFSCK to regard the system to be inconsistent, such as update the LAST_ID before (pre-)create the OST-objects.

there is no need in this if we do not let LFSCK to control LAST_ID

> From the OFD view, it is not always easily to detect the inconsistency by itself. For example, if the LAST_ID file crashed, and the OFD in handling the initialisation LAST_ID sync with MDT, then the expected "orphan cleanup" may become "pre-create". On the other hand, according to our recovery/replay mechanism, we allow the write relay to re-create OST-object. So even if the OST-object exists, the OFD-create cannot simply say that the system in inconsistency status.

all OFD needs to know is that some ID is available for new objects or not. strictly speaking we could implement a protocol which start with ID=0 on every boot and return available (not-in-use) IDs to MDT.

> When the LFSCK found inconsistency, it does not know whether it is the unique crashed LAST_ID file or there may be other ones until if finished the whole device scanning. So even if the OFD uses new sequence under inconsistency status, but if the LFSCK is paused before completed and resumes after the OST restart, it is also cannot easily to know whether the new allocated sequence is still valid to be used or not.

now imagine a user for a reason do not run LFSCK for quite a while...

Patch Set 9:

Patch Set 9:
>> For most of cases, the system will not be in inconsistent status, but the OFD also needs to handle carefully to avoid misguide the LFSCK to regard the system to be inconsistent, such as update the LAST_ID before (pre-)create the OST-objects.
> there is no need in this if we do not let LFSCK to control LAST_ID

What you mean? the LFSCK on OST does not check/repair LAST_ID?

>> From the OFD view, it is not always easily to detect the inconsistency by itself. For example, if the LAST_ID file crashed, and the OFD in handling the initialisation LAST_ID sync with MDT, then the expected "orphan cleanup" may become "pre-create". On the other hand, according to our recovery/replay mechanism, we allow the write relay to re-create OST-object. So even if the OST-object exists, the OFD-create cannot simply say that the system in inconsistency status.
> all OFD needs to know is that some ID is available for new objects or not. strictly speaking we could implement a protocol which start with ID=0 on every boot and return available (not-in-use) IDs to MDT.

Consider the huge sequence space and the MDT reboot frequency, your way can work on some degree. It simplifies not only the LFSCK but also the whole MDT-OST recovery. But it is out my control...

>> When the LFSCK found inconsistency, it does not know whether it is the unique crashed LAST_ID file or there may be other ones until if finished the whole device scanning. So even if the OFD uses new sequence under inconsistency status, but if the LFSCK is paused before completed and resumes after the OST restart, it is also cannot easily to know whether the new allocated sequence is still valid to be used or not.
> now imagine a user for a reason do not run LFSCK for quite a while...

It is true that the LFSCK maybe run much frequently. But these above corner cases should be considered unless as you suggested that using new FID sequence every time the MDT restart, then the LFSCK no need to care about the LAST_ID consistency...

Patch Set 9:

we don't need to start with a new sequence at every boot. we do already have a support in the protocol to reset precreate window (currently part of read-only -> scanning -> read-write sequence). all we need is to use this reset mechanism once OFD meets an existing object and switch to a new sequence.

Patch Set 9:

Alex Zhuravlev
1:04 PM
Patch Set 9:
> we don't need to start with a new sequence at every boot. we do already have a support in the protocol to reset precreate window (currently part of read-only -> scanning -> read-write sequence). all we need is to use this reset mechanism once OFD meets an existing object and switch to a new sequence.

If so, should we still make the LFSCK on OST to check/repair the LAST_ID files? Seems unnecessary.

Patch Set 9:

well, I can't say for sure. this is not something we have to change immediately, but it'd be good to consider this option at some point, IMHO.

Patch Set 9:

Alex or Fan Yong, can you please file a separate bug for 2.7.0 with details of how to change the LFSCK LAST_ID repair as was discussed here.

Patch Set 9: Code-Review+2

(1 comment)

Andreas, Alex: LU-5029 for further improvement.

parents.subject: LU-4972 lfsck: skip .lustre and children for namespace check

commit.subject: LU-4609 ofd: auto resume LFSCK after the recovery
commit.message:
LU-4609 ofd: auto resume LFSCK after the recovery

To prevent the LFSCK to recreate some objects which should be done
via some replayable RPCs, we will postpone the LFSCK auto assuming
after the server restart until the recovery finished.

Such serialization also avoid some race between LFSCK and recovery
to misguide the LFSCK to regard the system as inconsistent.

Another fix is that:
During the OST recovery, the client write RPC may create the missed
OST-object which will cause the real last_id to be greater than the
last_id value stored in the LAST_ID file temporarily. Normally, the
LAST_ID file will be synced between MDT and OST during the recovery,
but we should not assume that the MDT-OST recovery will succeed. If
it failed, then subsequent LFSCK on the OST may regard such LAST_ID
file as crashed, then it will mark the OST as read-only and rebuild
the LAST_ID files.

To avoid such case, before the write RPC to create the lost object,
it needs to update the LAST_ID file firstly.

Signed-off-by: Fan Yong <fan.yong@intel.com>
Change-Id: I963f3c1b70c7cad0c943f2485417d2e783768bf1
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/10010
Tested-by: Jenkins
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Alex Zhuravlev <alexey.zhuravlev@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
